questions.question: At what temperature do you need to call the transplant team right away?
questions.source_quote: Here's the rule: call for any single temperature over 100.4, or a temperature between 100.0 and 100.4 that lasts an hour or more.
questions.expected_answer: Call for any single temperature over 100.4, or a temperature between 100.0 and 100.4 lasting an hour or more.

questions.question: If you feel feverish, why shouldn't you just take Tylenol?
questions.source_quote: Don't wait, don't take Tylenol to hide any of it — call.
questions.expected_answer: Tylenol can hide the fever; you should call the team instead of masking it.

questions.question: What should you do if you start having chills or shaking, even without a fever?
questions.source_quote: if you get chills or start shaking, what we call rigors, call us.
questions.expected_answer: Call the team right away, because shaking/rigors can signal a serious infection even before a fever shows.

questions.question: What kind of rash should make you call the team, and where might you see it?
questions.source_quote: Call us right away for: a skin rash, especially on your palms or soles
questions.expected_answer: A new skin rash, especially on the palms or soles, means call the team.

questions.question: Besides fever and rash, what other new symptoms mean you should call us?
questions.source_quote: new diarrhea, yellowing of your eyes or skin, or a fever.
questions.expected_answer: New diarrhea or yellowing of the eyes or skin (jaundice) should prompt a call.

questions.question: How often and when should you take your tacrolimus each day?
questions.source_quote: You take it twice a day, about twelve hours apart — so think morning and evening, at the same times each day.
questions.expected_answer: Twice a day, about 12 hours apart, at the same times each day (morning and evening).

questions.question: Which food or drink must you avoid while taking tacrolimus, and why?
questions.source_quote: And do not take it with grapefruit or grapefruit juice, that can raise the level in your blood to a dangerous point.
questions.expected_answer: Avoid grapefruit and grapefruit juice because it can raise the tacrolimus level to a dangerous point.

questions.question: What should you do if you miss a dose of your tacrolimus?
questions.source_quote: So never double up if you miss a dose — just call us.
questions.expected_answer: Do not double up; call the team.

questions.question: What should you do if you throw up right after taking your medicine?
questions.source_quote: Also call us, don't just retake it on your own.
questions.expected_answer: Call the team; do not retake the dose on your own.

questions.question: Does it matter whether you take tacrolimus with food?
questions.source_quote: Just be consistent — if you take it with food, always take it with food.
questions.expected_answer: Be consistent — if taken with food, always take it with food.

questions.question: How often should you take your CellCept?
questions.source_quote: CellCept is usually taken every 8 hours.

questions.question: When do you need to wear a mask in public?
questions.source_quote: When your counts are low you'll wear a mask in public and around crowds — that infection and fungal risk is real.
questions.expected_answer: Wear a mask in public and around crowds when your counts are low, until the team says you can stop.

questions.question: What is the Bactrim for?
questions.source_quote: you'll also be on an antibiotic called Bactrim to prevent a specific pneumonia.
questions.expected_answer: Bactrim is an antibiotic to prevent a specific pneumonia.

questions.question: Can you explain what GVHD is and why you take these two medicines?
questions.source_quote: after your transplant, your new donor cells can sometimes attack your own body. That's called graft-versus-host disease, or GVHD. These two medicines are how we prevent that.
questions.expected_answer: GVHD is when the new donor cells attack the patient's own body; tacrolimus and CellCept help prevent it.

questions.question: What signs at home might mean the tacrolimus is affecting your kidneys?
questions.source_quote: If you're urinating a lot less, or your feet and ankles swell up, let us know.
questions.expected_answer: Urinating much less or swelling of the feet and ankles — report these to the team.
